title: The Morning Anomaly
setup: You are reviewing a peer-reviewed complexity proof in the faculty lounge before stand-up. A critical lemma in a graph-coloring algorithm contains a logical gap, and the PI will ask about it in ten minutes.
output: Lemma 4.2: If G is 3-colorable, then χ(G) ≤ 3 ∀v ∈ V. [ERROR: Contradicts counterexample v=7 in induced subgraph H]
prompt: What is your first instinct to handle the discrepancy?

A.text: Fire up a proof assistant (Coq/Lean) to formalize the lemma and isolate the exact logical break.
A.result: You are drawn to formal verification and pure theoretical repair—archetype: Theoretical CS Researcher.

B.text: Write a Python script to generate random graphs and stress-test the coloring algorithm empirically.
B.result: You prefer empirical validation and experimental systems—archetype: Experimental/Applied CS Researcher.

C.text: Draft a concise pedagogical note explaining the proof gap for your graduate seminar syllabus.
C.result: You prioritize knowledge dissemination and education—archetype: CS Educator & Science Communicator.

title: The Stand-Up Reckoning
setup: Your action confirmed the flaw is real. At the 11:00 lab stand-up, the director announces a funding deadline looms and asks how the lab should strategically position this discovery.
prompt: How do you propose the lab responds strategically?

A.text: Propose a new theoretical framework extending the proof with an added constraint model, seeking pure mathematics funding.
A.result: You push for deep theoretical advancement—archetype: Theoretical CS Researcher.

B.text: Pivot the grant narrative to benchmark real-world network failures caused by this algorithmic gap, targeting applied systems funding.
B.result: You leverage discovery for practical impact—archetype: Experimental/Applied CS Researcher.

C.text: Suggest an open-access workshop series and updated curriculum module to prevent future misuse in education.
C.result: You focus on institutional learning—archetype: CS Educator & Science Communicator.

title: The Grant Review
setup: The NSF-style review panel arrives at noon. They scrutinize your strategic proposal and demand deliverables aligned with the path you chose after the stand-up.
output: Deliverable Metrics: Theorem proofs (A), System benchmarks (B), MOOC enrollment & citation reach (C)
prompt: Which deliverable do you defend as the primary milestone?

A.text: Defend a 12-month proof roadmap with formal verification milestones and peer-reviewed conjectures.
A.result: You thrive in rigorous theoretical milestones—archetype: Theoretical CS Researcher.

B.text: Present a prototype detector running on live cluster traffic with reproducible benchmark datasets.
B.result: You value tangible, measurable systems—archetype: Experimental/Applied CS Researcher.

C.text: Show enrollment projections for the revised open curriculum and peer-review guidelines for textbooks.
C.result: You measure success by educational reach—archetype: CS Educator & Science Communicator.

title: The Replication Crisis
setup: At 3:00 PM, a panicked first-year PhD student finds their replication study broken—directly tied to the flawed theorem from this morning. The advisor is at a conference and you are the senior researcher present.
output: Traceback (replication): AssertionError: χ(G) != 3 in subgraph H (see Lemma 4.2).
prompt: How do you guide the student through the crisis?

A.text: Sit with them at the whiteboard to derive a corrected sub-lemma and salvage the theoretical contribution.
A.result: You mentor through rigorous logic—archetype: Theoretical CS Researcher.

B.text: Help them redesign the replication with control graphs, synthetic datasets, and statistical power analysis.
B.result: You mentor through experimental design—archetype: Experimental/Applied CS Researcher.

C.text: Encourage them to document the failure, write a replication report, and present it at the student journal club.
C.result: You mentor through scholarly communication—archetype: CS Educator & Science Communicator.

title: The Offer
setup: As evening falls, the department chair offers you a tenure-track research position shaped entirely by how you navigated the day's crisis, funding defense, and mentorship.
prompt: Which door do you enter to begin your permanent research career?

A.text: Accept leadership of the Formal Methods and Algorithms Group, pursuing tenure through peer-reviewed proofs and conjectures.
A.result: Your ideal path is pure theoretical discovery—archetype: Theoretical CS Professor.

B.text: Accept directorship of the Applied Systems and Benchmarking Lab, pursuing tenure through funded prototypes and open datasets.
B.result: Your ideal path is experimental systems impact—archetype: Experimental CS Research Director.

C.text: Accept founding directorship of the Center for CS Education and Open Scholarship, pursuing tenure through curriculum innovation and science communication.
C.result: Your ideal path is advancing CS through pedagogy—archetype: CS Education & Scholarship Leader.
